back_translation: A Maskil of Asaph. O my people, give ear to my instruction; listen to the words of my mouth.
I will open my mouth in a parable; I will utter things hidden from ancient times,
things that we have heard and known, and that our fathers have told us.
We will not hide them from their children, but will tell the next generation the praiseworthy deeds of the LORD, His might, and the wonders He has done.
For He established a testimony in Jacob and appointed a law in Israel, which He commanded our fathers to teach to their children,
so that the next generation would know them—children yet to be born—and would rise up and tell their own children,
so that they would put their trust in God, not forgetting His works but keeping His commandments,
and would not be like their fathers, a stubborn and rebellious generation, whose heart was not loyal and whose spirit was not faithful to God.
The men of Ephraim, armed with bows, turned back on the day of battle.
They did not keep God's covenant and refused to walk in His law.
They forgot what He had done and the wonders He had shown them.
He worked wonders before their fathers in the land of Egypt, in the region of Zoan.
He divided the sea and led them through; He made the waters stand up like a wall.
He led them with a cloud by day and with the light of fire all night.
He split the rocks in the wilderness and gave them water to drink as abundant as the seas.
He made streams flow out of the rock and made water run down like rivers.
But they continued to sin against Him, rebelling against the Most High in the wilderness.
They willfully tested God by demanding the food they craved.
They spoke against God, saying, 'Can God prepare a table in the wilderness?
True, when He struck the rock, water gushed out and streams flowed; but can He also give bread or provide meat for His people?'
Therefore, when the LORD heard, He was filled with wrath; fire flared up against Jacob, and His anger rose against Israel,
because they did not believe God or trust in His salvation.
Yet He commanded the clouds above and opened the doors of the heavens.
He rained down manna for them to eat and gave them grain from heaven.
Man ate the bread of angels; He sent them food in abundance.
He made the east wind blow from the heavens and led out the south wind by His might.
He rained meat on them like dust, and winged birds like the sand of the sea.
He made it fall in the midst of their camp, all around their dwellings.
So they ate and were well filled, for He gave them what they craved.
But before they had satisfied their craving, while the food was still in their mouths,
the anger of God flared up against them; He killed their strongest men and subdued the young men of Israel.
In spite of all this, they kept on sinning; despite His wonderful works, they still did not believe.
So He made their days end in futility and their years in sudden terror.
When He killed them, they sought Him; they repented and earnestly sought God.
They remembered that God was their Rock, and that God Most High was their Redeemer.
But they deceived Him with their mouths and lied to Him with their tongues.
Their hearts were not loyal to Him, and they were not faithful to His covenant.
Yet He, full of compassion, forgave their iniquity and did not destroy them. Many times He restrained His anger and did not unleash all His wrath.
He remembered that they were but flesh, a wind that passes and does not return.
How often they disobeyed Him in the wilderness and grieved Him in the desert!
Again and again they tested God and provoked the Holy One of Israel.
They did not remember His power, the day He redeemed them from the enemy,
when He displayed His signs in Egypt and His wonders in the fields of Zoan.
He turned their rivers to blood, so they could not drink from their streams.
He sent swarms of flies that devoured them, and frogs that destroyed them.
He gave their crops to the young locust and the fruit of their labor to the locust.
He destroyed their vines with hail and their sycamore-figs with frost.
He gave over their cattle to the hail and their livestock to bolts of lightning.
He unleashed His burning anger against them—wrath, indignation, and calamity—a band of destroying angels.
He made a path for His anger; He did not spare them from death but gave their lives over to the plague.
He struck down all the firstborn of Egypt, the firstfruits of their strength in the tents of Ham.
Then He led out His people like sheep and guided them like a flock in the wilderness.
He led them safely, so they were not afraid; but the sea engulfed their enemies.
He brought them to His holy land, to the mountain His right hand had acquired.
He drove out nations before them and apportioned their land as an inheritance; He settled the tribes of Israel in their tents.
But they tested and disobeyed God Most High, and did not keep His decrees.
They turned back and were faithless like their fathers; they were unreliable like a faulty bow.
They provoked Him to anger with their high places and to jealousy with their idols.
When God heard this, He was furious and rejected Israel completely.
He abandoned the tabernacle at Shiloh, the tent He had set up among men.
He gave the ark of His strength into captivity, and His splendor into the hand of the enemy.
He gave His people over to the sword, because He was furious with His inheritance.
Fire consumed their young men, and their maidens had no wedding songs.
Their priests fell by the sword, and their widows could not weep.
Then the Lord awoke as from sleep, like a warrior reviving from wine.
He drove His enemies back and put them to everlasting shame.
He rejected the tent of Joseph and did not choose the tribe of Ephraim.
But He chose the tribe of Judah, Mount Zion, which He loved.
He built His sanctuary to tower high, like the earth He has established forever.
He chose David His servant and took him from the sheepfolds;
He brought him from tending the ewes to be the shepherd of Jacob His people, and Israel His inheritance.
So David shepherded them with integrity of heart and guided them with skillful hands.
